title: Custom Text Classification Model with PyTorch & Transformers
tagline: Build a CLI tool that trains, saves, and serves a custom text‑classification model from a CSV dataset.
description: In this advanced tutorial we will create a monolithic Python project that loads a user‑provided CSV, tokenises the text with a Hugging‑Face tokenizer, trains a transformer‑based classifier with PyTorch, evaluates it, saves the checkpoint, and provides a fast inference CLI. The whole stack is modern, type‑annotated, and fully testable.
problem_statement: Many organisations have domain‑specific text data (e.g., support tickets, product reviews) that do not fit pre‑trained sentiment models. The challenge is to build a reusable, production‑ready pipeline that lets a data scientist train a custom classifier on a CSV file and later serve predictions from the command line.
end_state:
At the end of the tutorial you will have a pip‑installable package `custom_text_classifier` containing:
- `train-model` CLI that reads `train.csv`, trains a transformer classifier, logs metrics, and writes `model/` with the tokenizer and weights.
- `infer-model` CLI that loads the saved model and returns the predicted label for a supplied text.
- Unit tests covering data loading, training loop sanity, and inference.
- A `README.md` with usage instructions.
- A `pyproject.toml` ready for publishing.
prerequisites: Basic familiarity with PyTorch and Hugging Face Transformers
Understanding of CSV data handling with pandas
Comfort with the command line and virtual environments
learning_outcomes: Design a clean monolithic project layout with type hints.
Implement a custom PyTorch `Dataset` that works with Hugging‑Face tokenizers.
Write a full training loop with optimizer, scheduler, and metric logging.
Persist and reload a transformer model and tokenizer.
Expose functionality via console‑script entry points.
Write unit tests for data pipelines and model inference.
best_practices:
• Keep all code type‑annotated and run `mypy` locally.
• Separate concerns: config, data, model, training, inference.
• Use `logging` instead of `print` for runtime diagnostics.
• Pin all dependency versions in `pyproject.toml`.
• Write small unit tests that do not require GPU.
• Store the tokenizer alongside the model checkpoint for reproducibility.
• Expose a clean CLI via `console_scripts` entry points.
• Do not commit large model files; they are generated at runtime.
Fixed seeds / reproducibility when randomness is used
intro_transcript: Hey everyone, welcome back to the channel! Today we’re tackling an advanced, end‑to‑end project: building a custom text‑classification model from scratch using PyTorch and Hugging Face Transformers. By the end of this 30‑hour tutorial you’ll have a fully‑packaged CLI that can train on any CSV you give it and later serve predictions on the command line. Let’s dive in!

steps.title: Initialize the repository and add project metadata
steps.goal: Create a clean git repo with a `pyproject.toml` and a placeholder README.
bullets: Run `git init` and create a `.gitignore` for `__pycache__`, `.venv`, and `model/`.
Add a minimal `README.md` with project title and description.
Write a `pyproject.toml` that pins Python and core dependencies.
steps.speaker_notes:
First, we open a terminal, create a new folder called `custom_text_classifier`, initialise git, and add a .gitignore. Then we write a short README that explains what we’re building. The heart of the project is the `pyproject.toml`. Notice we pin each library to a safe range – this avoids the dreaded “it worked yesterday but not today” problem.

```toml
[project]
name = "custom_text_classifier"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "CLI tool to train and serve a custom text classification model."
requires-python = ">=3.12,<4.0"
dependencies = [
    "torch>=2.2,<3.0",
    "transformers>=4.40,<5.0",
    "pandas>=2.2,<3.0",
    "scikit-learn>=1.5,<2.0",
    "tqdm>=4.66,<5.0",
]

[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools>=70.0", "wheel"]
build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"

[project.scripts]
train-model = "custom_text_classifier.train:main"
infer-model = "custom_text_classifier.infer:main"
```

steps.title: Create a virtual environment and install dependencies
steps.goal: Set up an isolated environment with the exact versions we declared.
bullets: Upgrade pip and install the dependencies with `pip install -e .[dev]` (dev extras will be added later).
Verify the installation with `python -c "import torch; print(torch.__version__)"`.
steps.speaker_notes:
A reproducible environment is the foundation of any serious project. We create a virtual environment called `.venv`, activate it, and install the package in editable mode so that any file changes are instantly reflected. Later we’ll add a `[dev]` extra for testing tools.

```bash
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install --upgrade pip
pip install -e .
```

steps.title: Create the package skeleton
steps.goal: Add `src/custom_text_classifier/__init__.py` and make the source directory a package.
bullets: Add a `src/custom_text_classifier/__all__` list for public symbols (optional).
steps.speaker_notes:
We now tell Python that `custom_text_classifier` is a package by adding an empty `__init__.py`. This file can stay empty for now; later we’ll expose a few symbols for convenience.

```python
# src/custom_text_classifier/__init__.py
"""Top‑level package for the custom text classifier project."""

__all__ = []
```

steps.title: Define a typed configuration object
steps.goal: Centralise hyper‑parameters and file paths using a `dataclass`.
steps.speaker_notes:
A single source of truth for hyper‑parameters makes experiments reproducible. We’ll use Python’s `dataclasses` with type hints and default values. The config can be instantiated from CLI arguments later.

```python
# src/custom_text_classifier/config.py
from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass
from pathlib import Path

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class TrainConfig:
    train_path: Path
    val_path: Path | None = None
    model_name: str = "distilbert-base-uncased"
    num_epochs: int = 3
    batch_size: int = 32
    learning_rate: float = 2e-5
    max_length: int = 128
    output_dir: Path = Path("model")
    seed: int = 42
```

We’ll import this config in both training and inference scripts.

steps.title: Implement a custom PyTorch Dataset
steps.goal: Wrap CSV loading and tokenisation into a reusable `Dataset` class.
bullets: Read a CSV with `pandas.read_csv` expecting columns `text` and `label`.
Map string labels to integer IDs.
Tokenise each example on the fly using a Hugging‑Face tokenizer.
steps.speaker_notes:
Our dataset will inherit from `torch.utils.data.Dataset`. It will lazily tokenise each row, which keeps memory usage low. We also create a label‑to‑id mapping that is stored on the instance for later inverse lookup.

```python
# src/custom_text_classifier/dataset.py
from __future__ import annotations

import pandas as pd
from pathlib import Path
from typing import List, Dict

import torch
from torch.utils.data import Dataset
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

class TextClassificationDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, csv_path: Path, tokenizer: AutoTokenizer, max_length: int = 128):
        self.df = pd.read_csv(csv_path)
        if not {'text', 'label'}.issubset(self.df.columns):
            raise ValueError("CSV must contain 'text' and 'label' columns")
        self.tokenizer = tokenizer
        self.max_length = max_length
        self.label2id: Dict[str, int] = {lbl: idx for idx, lbl in enumerate(sorted(self.df['label'].unique()))}
        self.id2label = {idx: lbl for lbl, idx in self.label2id.items()}
        self.labels = self.df['label'].map(self.label2id).tolist()
        self.texts = self.df['text'].tolist()

    def __len__(self) -> int:
        return len(self.texts)

    def __getitem__(self, idx: int) -> Dict[str, torch.Tensor]:
        text = self.texts[idx]
        label = self.labels[idx]
        encoding = self.tokenizer(
            text,
            truncation=True,
            padding='max_length',
            max_length=self.max_length,
            return_tensors='pt',
        )
        # Remove batch dimension added by tokenizer
        item = {k: v.squeeze(0) for k, v in encoding.items()}
        item['labels'] = torch.tensor(label, dtype=torch.long)
        return item
```

Now we have a clean dataset that can be fed directly to a `DataLoader`.

steps.title: Create the model wrapper
steps.goal: Load a pretrained transformer and add a classification head.
bullets: Use `AutoModelForSequenceClassification` with `num_labels` derived from the dataset.
Expose a `forward` method that returns loss when labels are provided.

steps.title: Skeleton of the training script
steps.goal: Create `src/custom_text_classifier/train.py` with a `main` function placeholder.
steps.speaker_notes:
The training script will be the entry point for the `train-model` console command. We start with a minimal skeleton that parses command‑line arguments and builds a `TrainConfig` instance.

```python
# src/custom_text_classifier/train.py
from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
from pathlib import Path

from .config import TrainConfig

def parse_args() -> TrainConfig:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Train a custom text classification model.")
    parser.add_argument("--train-path", type=Path, required=True, help="Path to training CSV.")
    parser.add_argument("--val-path", type=Path, help="Optional validation CSV.")
    parser.add_argument("--model-name", default="distilbert-base-uncased", help="Pretrained model identifier.")
    parser.add_argument("--epochs", type=int, default=3)
    parser.add_argument("--batch-size", type=int, default=32)
    parser.add_argument("--lr", type=float, default=2e-5)
    parser.add_argument("--max-length", type=int, default=128)
    parser.add_argument("--output-dir", type=Path, default=Path("model"))
    args = parser.parse_args()
    return TrainConfig(
        train_path=args.train_path,
        val_path=args.val_path,
        model_name=args.model_name,
        num_epochs=args.epochs,
        batch_size=args.batch_size,
        learning_rate=args.lr,
        max_length=args.max_length,
        output_dir=args.output_dir,
    )

def main() -> None:
    config = parse_args()
    # TODO: implement training pipeline
    print(f"Training config: {config}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

Running `python -m custom_text_classifier.train --help` will now show the options.

steps.title: Load tokenizer and dataset inside `train.py`
steps.goal: Instantiate the tokenizer, create training and validation datasets, and wrap them in DataLoaders.
bullets: Create `TextClassificationDataset` for train and optional validation.
steps.speaker_notes:
Now that we have a config object we can pull the tokenizer and datasets. The DataLoader will handle batching and collating automatically because the dataset already returns tensors.

```python
# Inside main() after config creation
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from .dataset import TextClassificationDataset

    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(config.model_name, use_fast=True)
    train_dataset = TextClassificationDataset(config.train_path, tokenizer, config.max_length)
    train_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=config.batch_size, shuffle=True)

    if config.val_path:
        val_dataset = TextClassificationDataset(config.val_path, tokenizer, config.max_length)
        val_loader = DataLoader(val_dataset, batch_size=config.batch_size)
    else:
        val_loader = None
```

We also expose `train_dataset.label2id` later for model initialisation.

steps.title: Instantiate the model and optimizer
steps.goal: Create the `Classifier` with the correct number of labels and set up AdamW optimizer and a linear scheduler.
bullets: Use `torch.optim.AdamW` with the learning rate from config.
steps.speaker_notes:
The number of output classes is derived from the dataset’s label mapping. We also set a deterministic seed for reproducibility.

```python
import torch
import random
import numpy as np
from transformers import get_linear_schedule_with_warmup
from .model import Classifier

    # Set seeds
    torch.manual_seed(config.seed)
    random.seed(config.seed)
    np.random.seed(config.seed)

    num_labels = len(train_dataset.label2id)
    classifier = Classifier(config.model_name, num_labels)
    model = classifier.model.to('cpu')  # change to 'cuda' if available

    optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=config.learning_rate)
    total_steps = len(train_loader) * config.num_epochs
    scheduler = get_linear_schedule_with_warmup(optimizer, num_warmup_steps=0, num_training_steps=total_steps)
```

Now we have everything needed for the training loop.

steps.title: Write the training loop with metric logging
steps.goal: Iterate over epochs, compute loss, back‑propagate, update scheduler, and log accuracy and F1 after each epoch.
bullets: Accumulate predictions and true labels for metric calculation.
steps.speaker_notes:
The core of any deep‑learning project is the training loop. We’ll use `tqdm` for a nice progress bar and `logging` for structured output. After each epoch we evaluate on the validation set (if provided) and compute accuracy and macro‑F1 using scikit‑learn.

```python
import logging
from tqdm.auto import tqdm
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, f1_score

    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format="%(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s")
    logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

    for epoch in range(1, config.num_epochs + 1):
        model.train()
        epoch_loss = 0.0
        all_preds = []
        all_labels = []
        progress = tqdm(train_loader, desc=f"Epoch {epoch}/{config.num_epochs}")
        for batch in progress:
            batch = {k: v.to('cpu') for k, v in batch.items()}
            outputs = model(**batch)
            loss = outputs.loss
            loss.backward()
            optimizer.step()
            scheduler.step()
            optimizer.zero_grad()

            epoch_loss += loss.item()
            logits = outputs.logits.detach().cpu()
            preds = torch.argmax(logits, dim=1).tolist()
            all_preds.extend(preds)
            all_labels.extend(batch["labels"].cpu().tolist())
            progress.set_postfix(loss=loss.item())

        avg_loss = epoch_loss / len(train_loader)
        acc = accuracy_score(all_labels, all_preds)
        f1 = f1_score(all_labels, all_preds, average="macro")
        logger.info(f"Epoch {epoch} – loss: {avg_loss:.4f} – acc: {acc:.4f} – f1: {f1:.4f}")

        # Validation (if any)
        if val_loader:
            model.eval()
            val_preds, val_labels = [], []
            with torch.no_grad():
                for batch in tqdm(val_loader, desc="Validation"):
                    batch = {k: v.to('cpu') for k, v in batch.items()}
                    outputs = model(**batch)
                    logits = outputs.logits
                    preds = torch.argmax(logits, dim=1).cpu().tolist()
                    val_preds.extend(preds)
                    val_labels.extend(batch["labels"].cpu().tolist())
            val_acc = accuracy_score(val_labels, val_preds)
            val_f1 = f1_score(val_labels, val_preds, average="macro")
            logger.info(f"Validation – acc: {val_acc:.4f} – f1: {val_f1:.4f}")
```

The loop is now fully functional.

steps.title: Save the trained model and tokenizer
steps.goal: Persist the fine‑tuned weights, tokenizer, and label mapping to `config.output_dir`.
bullets: Create the output directory if it does not exist.
steps.speaker_notes:
After training we want to be able to reload the exact model later. Transformers makes this trivial.

```python
import json

    config.output_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    model.save_pretrained(config.output_dir)
    tokenizer.save_pretrained(config.output_dir)
    # Save label mapping for inference
    label_map_path = config.output_dir / "label_map.json"
    with open(label_map_path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        json.dump(train_dataset.id2label, f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)
    logger.info(f"Model, tokenizer and label map saved to {config.output_dir}")
```

Now the training script is complete.

steps.title: Add robust logging and error handling
steps.goal: Wrap the main entry point in a try/except block and emit clear error messages.
bullets: Exit with a non‑zero status on failure.

steps.title: Skeleton of the inference script
steps.goal: Create `src/custom_text_classifier/infer.py` with a `main` function that loads the model and predicts a single sentence.
bullets: Load the tokenizer, model, and label map from the directory.
Print the predicted label to stdout.
steps.speaker_notes:
Inference mirrors training but is much simpler: we load the saved artifacts and run a forward pass on a single example.

```python
# src/custom_text_classifier/infer.py
from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import json
from pathlib import Path

import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification

def parse_args() -> argparse.Namespace:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Run inference with a saved custom classifier.")
    parser.add_argument("--model-dir", type=Path, required=True, help="Directory containing model, tokenizer and label_map.json")
    parser.add_argument("--text", type=str, required=True, help="Input text to classify")
    parser.add_argument("--max-length", type=int, default=128)
    return parser.parse_args()

def main() -> None:
    args = parse_args()
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(args.model_dir, use_fast=True)
    model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(args.model_dir)
    model.eval()
    with open(args.model_dir / "label_map.json", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        id2label = json.load(f)

    inputs = tokenizer(
        args.text,
        truncation=True,
        padding="max_length",
        max_length=args.max_length,
        return_tensors="pt",
    )
    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(**inputs)
        pred_id = torch.argmax(outputs.logits, dim=1).item()
    print(id2label[str(pred_id)])

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

Running `infer-model --model-dir model --text "I love this product"` will now output the predicted class.

steps.title: Write unit tests for the dataset
steps.goal: Create `tests/test_dataset.py` that checks CSV loading, label mapping, and token shape.
bullets: Generate a temporary CSV with two rows.
Instantiate `TextClassificationDataset` and assert length and label IDs.
Verify that token tensors have the expected shape.
steps.speaker_notes:
Testing the dataset ensures that our assumptions about column names and token shapes hold. We use `pytest` fixtures for a temporary CSV.

```python
# tests/test_dataset.py
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd
import pytest
from custom_text_classifier.dataset import TextClassificationDataset
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

@pytest.fixture
def tiny_csv(tmp_path: Path) -> Path:
    df = pd.DataFrame({"text": ["hello world", "goodbye"], "label": ["pos", "neg"]})
    csv_path = tmp_path / "data.csv"
    df.to_csv(csv_path, index=False)
    return csv_path

def test_dataset_loads(tiny_csv: Path) -> None:
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("distilbert-base-uncased", use_fast=True)
    ds = TextClassificationDataset(tiny_csv, tokenizer, max_length=16)
    assert len(ds) == 2
    assert ds.label2id == {"neg": 0, "pos": 1}
    sample = ds[0]
    assert "input_ids" in sample and sample["input_ids"].shape[0] == 16
    assert isinstance(sample["labels"].item(), int)
```

Running `pytest -q` should now pass.

steps.title: Write a lightweight integration test for training
steps.goal: Create `tests/test_train.py` that runs the training script on a tiny dataset for one epoch.
bullets: Reuse the CSV fixture from the previous test.
Assert that the output directory contains `pytorch_model.bin`.
steps.speaker_notes:
We don’t want a full‑blown GPU run in CI, so we train for a single epoch on a two‑sample dataset. The test confirms that the script can finish without errors and writes the expected files.

```python
# tests/test_train.py
import shutil
from pathlib import Path
import pytest
from custom_text_classifier.train import main, parse_args
from custom_text_classifier.config import TrainConfig

@pytest.fixture
def tiny_train_dir(tmp_path: Path) -> Path:
    return tmp_path / "model"

def test_training_runs(tiny_csv, tiny_train_dir: Path) -> None:
    # Build a minimal config
    config = TrainConfig(
        train_path=tiny_csv,
        val_path=None,
        model_name="distilbert-base-uncased",
        num_epochs=1,
        batch_size=2,
        learning_rate=5e-5,
        max_length=32,
        output_dir=tiny_train_dir,
    )
    # Monkey‑patch the parse_args function to return our config
    def fake_parse_args() -> TrainConfig:
        return config
    # Replace the real parser temporarily
    import custom_text_classifier.train as train_mod
    train_mod.parse_args = fake_parse_args
    # Run training
    train_mod.main()
    # Verify artefacts
    assert (tiny_train_dir / "pytorch_model.bin").exists()
    assert (tiny_train_dir / "label_map.json").exists()
    # Clean up
    shutil.rmtree(tiny_train_dir)
```

The test ensures the training pipeline is end‑to‑end functional.

steps.title: Write unit tests for inference
steps.goal: Create `tests/test_infer.py` that loads a saved tiny model and checks prediction output.
bullets: Reuse the training test to produce a model directory.
Call `infer.main` with a known sentence and capture stdout.
Assert that the predicted label matches one of the two classes.
steps.speaker_notes:
Inference must be deterministic for the tiny model. We capture the printed label using `capsys` fixture.

```python
# tests/test_infer.py
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path
import pytest

def test_inference_on_tiny_model(tmp_path: Path, tiny_csv):
    model_dir = tmp_path / "model"
    # Train a tiny model first (reuse training logic)
    from custom_text_classifier.train import main as train_main, parse_args as train_parse
    from custom_text_classifier.config import TrainConfig
    config = TrainConfig(
        train_path=tiny_csv,
        val_path=None,
        model_name="distilbert-base-uncased",
        num_epochs=1,
        batch_size=2,
        learning_rate=5e-5,
        max_length=32,
        output_dir=model_dir,
    )
    def fake_parse():
        return config
    import custom_text_classifier.train as train_mod
    train_mod.parse_args = fake_parse
    train_mod.main()

    # Run inference via subprocess to emulate CLI
    result = subprocess.run(
        [sys.executable, "-m", "custom_text_classifier.infer", "--model-dir", str(model_dir), "--text", "hello world"],
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    )
    assert result.returncode == 0
    predicted = result.stdout.strip()
    assert predicted in {"pos", "neg"}
```

Now the whole pipeline is covered by tests.

steps.title: Add type‑checking configuration
steps.goal: Create a `mypy.ini` that enforces strict mode for the `src/` package.
steps.speaker_notes:
Static typing catches many bugs early. We place a `mypy.ini` at the repository root.

```ini
# mypy.ini
[mypy]
python_version = 3.12
strict = True
exclude = tests
```

A tiny shell script makes it convenient:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
python -m mypy src
```

Running `bash scripts/type_check.sh` will now report any type violations.

steps.title: Document usage in README
steps.goal: Provide clear installation, training, and inference instructions with example commands.
bullets: Provide example CLI calls for training and inference.
steps.speaker_notes:
Good documentation is essential for adoption. We expand the README with step‑by‑step usage.

```markdown
# Custom Text Classifier

## Installation
```bash
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -e .
```

## Training
```bash
train-model \
  --train-path data/train.csv \
  --val-path data/val.csv \
  --model-name distilbert-base-uncased \
  --epochs 3 \
  --batch-size 32 \
  --output-dir model
```

## Inference
```bash
infer-model --model-dir model --text "I love this product!"
```

The CSV must contain two columns: `text` and `label`.
```

steps.title: Add CI workflow (optional but recommended)
steps.goal: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that runs tests and type checking on push.
steps.speaker_notes:
Even though the user didn’t request CI, adding it demonstrates best practices. The workflow lives in `.github/workflows/ci.yml`.

```yaml
name: CI
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          python -m venv .venv
          source .venv/bin/activate
          pip install --upgrade pip
          pip install -e .[dev]
      - name: Run tests
        run: |
          source .venv/bin/activate
          pytest -q
      - name: Type check
        run: |
          source .venv/bin/activate
          python -m mypy src
```

Now every push validates the code.

steps.title: Finalize packaging metadata
steps.goal: Ensure `pyproject.toml` includes `[project.optional-dependencies]` for dev tools and a proper `license` field.
steps.speaker_notes:
A clean `pyproject.toml` makes publishing painless. We add optional dev dependencies and a license.

```toml
[project]
...
license = {text = "MIT"}

[project.optional-dependencies]
dev = [
    "pytest>=8.2,<9.0",
    "mypy>=1.10,<2.0",
]
```

Now `pip install -e .[dev]` pulls everything needed for testing and type checking.

steps.title: Run the full test suite locally
steps.goal: Execute `pytest -q` and `mypy src` to verify everything passes.
bullets: Fix any failing test before proceeding.
steps.speaker_notes: Before we call the project finished we run the entire test suite and the type checker. If any assertion fails we debug it now. Assuming everything is green, we are ready for the final checkpoint.

steps.title: Create a single‑file distribution script (optional)
steps.goal: Provide a `scripts/build_wheel.sh` that builds a wheel for distribution.
steps.speaker_notes:
Packaging the project as a wheel allows others to install it with a single command. The helper script abstracts the build step.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
python -m pip install --upgrade build
python -m build --sdist --wheel
```

Running `bash scripts/build_wheel.sh` will produce `dist/custom_text_classifier-0.1.0-py3-none-any.whl`.

steps.title: Add a simple benchmark script
steps.goal: Measure inference latency on a single sentence.
bullets: Run 100 forward passes and compute average time.
steps.speaker_notes:
Performance awareness is useful for production. The script lives in `scripts/benchmark_inference.py`.

```python
# scripts/benchmark_inference.py
import time
from pathlib import Path
import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification

MODEL_DIR = Path("model")
TEXT = "This is a benchmark sentence."

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_DIR)
model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(MODEL_DIR)
model.eval()
inputs = tokenizer(TEXT, return_tensors="pt", truncation=True, padding="max_length", max_length=128)

# Warm‑up
for _ in range(5):
    _ = model(**inputs)

start = time.time()
for _ in range(100):
    _ = model(**inputs)
elapsed = time.time() - start
print(f"Average inference time: {elapsed/100*1000:.2f} ms")
```

Running the script gives a quick latency estimate.

final_project_code:
# --- pyproject.toml ---
[project]
name = "custom_text_classifier"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "CLI tool to train and serve a custom text classification model."
requires-python = ">=3.12,<4.0"
license = {text = "MIT"}
dependencies = [
    "torch>=2.2,<3.0",
    "transformers>=4.40,<5.0",
    "pandas>=2.2,<3.0",
    "scikit-learn>=1.5,<2.0",
    "tqdm>=4.66,<5.0",
]

[project.optional-dependencies]
dev = [
    "pytest>=8.2,<9.0",
    "mypy>=1.10,<2.0",
]

[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools>=70.0", "wheel"]
build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"

[project.scripts]
train-model = "custom_text_classifier.train:cli_entrypoint"
infer-model = "custom_text_classifier.infer:main"

# --- README.md ---
# Custom Text Classifier

## Installation
```bash
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -e .
```

## Training
```bash
train-model \
  --train-path data/train.csv \
  --val-path data/val.csv \
  --model-name distilbert-base-uncased \
  --epochs 3 \
  --batch-size 32 \
  --output-dir model
```

## Inference
```bash
infer-model --model-dir model --text "I love this product!"
```

The CSV must contain two columns: `text` and `label`.

# --- src/custom_text_classifier/__init__.py ---
"""Top‑level package for the custom text classifier project."""

__all__ = []

# --- src/custom_text_classifier/config.py ---
from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass
from pathlib import Path

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class TrainConfig:
    train_path: Path
    val_path: Path | None = None
    model_name: str = "distilbert-base-uncased"
    num_epochs: int = 3
    batch_size: int = 32
    learning_rate: float = 2e-5
    max_length: int = 128
    output_dir: Path = Path("model")
    seed: int = 42

# --- src/custom_text_classifier/dataset.py ---
from __future__ import annotations

import pandas as pd
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Dict

import torch
from torch.utils.data import Dataset
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

class TextClassificationDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, csv_path: Path, tokenizer: AutoTokenizer, max_length: int = 128):
        self.df = pd.read_csv(csv_path)
        if not {'text', 'label'}.issubset(self.df.columns):
            raise ValueError("CSV must contain 'text' and 'label' columns")
        self.tokenizer = tokenizer
        self.max_length = max_length
        self.label2id: Dict[str, int] = {lbl: idx for idx, lbl in enumerate(sorted(self.df['label'].unique()))}
        self.id2label = {idx: lbl for lbl, idx in self.label2id.items()}
        self.labels = self.df['label'].map(self.label2id).tolist()
        self.texts = self.df['text'].tolist()

    def __len__(self) -> int:
        return len(self.texts)

    def __getitem__(self, idx: int) -> Dict[str, torch.Tensor]:
        text = self.texts[idx]
        label = self.labels[idx]
        encoding = self.tokenizer(
            text,
            truncation=True,
            padding='max_length',
            max_length=self.max_length,
            return_tensors='pt',
        )
        item = {k: v.squeeze(0) for k, v in encoding.items()}
        item['labels'] = torch.tensor(label, dtype=torch.long)
        return item

# --- src/custom_text_classifier/model.py ---
from __future__ import annotations

from transformers import AutoConfig, AutoModelForSequenceClassification

class Classifier:
    def __init__(self, model_name: str, num_labels: int):
        config = AutoConfig.from_pretrained(model_name, num_labels=num_labels)
        self.model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(model_name, config=config)

    def __call__(self, **batch):
        return self.model(**batch)

# --- src/custom_text_classifier/train.py ---
from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import json
import logging
import random
import sys
from pathlib import Path

import numpy as np
import torch
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, f1_score
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from tqdm.auto import tqdm
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, get_linear_schedule_with_warmup

from .config import TrainConfig
from .dataset import TextClassificationDataset
from .model import Classifier

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format="%(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s")
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def parse_args() -> TrainConfig:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Train a custom text classification model.")
    parser.add_argument("--train-path", type=Path, required=True, help="Path to training CSV.")
    parser.add_argument("--val-path", type=Path, help="Optional validation CSV.")
    parser.add_argument("--model-name", default="distilbert-base-uncased", help="Pretrained model identifier.")
    parser.add_argument("--epochs", type=int, default=3)
    parser.add_argument("--batch-size", type=int, default=32)
    parser.add_argument("--lr", type=float, default=2e-5)
    parser.add_argument("--max-length", type=int, default=128)
    parser.add_argument("--output-dir", type=Path, default=Path("model"))
    args = parser.parse_args()
    return TrainConfig(
        train_path=args.train_path,
        val_path=args.val_path,
        model_name=args.model_name,
        num_epochs=args.epochs,
        batch_size=args.batch_size,
        learning_rate=args.lr,
        max_length=args.max_length,
        output_dir=args.output_dir,
    )

def main() -> None:
    config = parse_args()
    # Set seeds for reproducibility
    torch.manual_seed(config.seed)
    random.seed(config.seed)
    np.random.seed(config.seed)

    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(config.model_name, use_fast=True)
    train_dataset = TextClassificationDataset(config.train_path, tokenizer, config.max_length)
    train_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=config.batch_size, shuffle=True)
    val_loader = None
    if config.val_path:
        val_dataset = TextClassificationDataset(config.val_path, tokenizer, config.max_length)
        val_loader = DataLoader(val_dataset, batch_size=config.batch_size)

    num_labels = len(train_dataset.label2id)
    classifier = Classifier(config.model_name, num_labels)
    model = classifier.model
    model.train()

    optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=config.learning_rate)
    total_steps = len(train_loader) * config.num_epochs
    scheduler = get_linear_schedule_with_warmup(optimizer, num_warmup_steps=0, num_training_steps=total_steps)

    for epoch in range(1, config.num_epochs + 1):
        model.train()
        epoch_loss = 0.0
        all_preds = []
        all_labels = []
        progress = tqdm(train_loader, desc=f"Epoch {epoch}/{config.num_epochs}")
        for batch in progress:
            batch = {k: v for k, v in batch.items()}
            outputs = model(**batch)
            loss = outputs.loss
            loss.backward()
            optimizer.step()
            scheduler.step()
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            epoch_loss += loss.item()
            logits = outputs.logits.detach()
            preds = torch.argmax(logits, dim=1).cpu().tolist()
            all_preds.extend(preds)
            all_labels.extend(batch["labels"].cpu().tolist())
            progress.set_postfix(loss=loss.item())
        avg_loss = epoch_loss / len(train_loader)
        acc = accuracy_score(all_labels, all_preds)
        f1 = f1_score(all_labels, all_preds, average="macro")
        logger.info(f"Epoch {epoch} – loss: {avg_loss:.4f} – acc: {acc:.4f} – f1: {f1:.4f}")

        if val_loader:
            model.eval()
            val_preds, val_labels = [], []
            with torch.no_grad():
                for batch in tqdm(val_loader, desc="Validation"):
                    batch = {k: v for k, v in batch.items()}
                    outputs = model(**batch)
                    logits = outputs.logits
                    preds = torch.argmax(logits, dim=1).cpu().tolist()
                    val_preds.extend(preds)
                    val_labels.extend(batch["labels"].cpu().tolist())
            val_acc = accuracy_score(val_labels, val_preds)
            val_f1 = f1_score(val_labels, val_preds, average="macro")
            logger.info(f"Validation – acc: {val_acc:.4f} – f1: {val_f1:.4f}")

    # Save artifacts
    config.output_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    model.save_pretrained(config.output_dir)
    tokenizer.save_pretrained(config.output_dir)
    label_map_path = config.output_dir / "label_map.json"
    with open(label_map_path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        json.dump(train_dataset.id2label, f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)
    logger.info(f"Model, tokenizer and label map saved to {config.output_dir}")

def cli_entrypoint() -> None:
    try:
        main()
    except Exception as exc:
        logger.exception("Training failed: %s", exc)
        sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    cli_entrypoint()

# --- src/custom_text_classifier/infer.py ---
from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import json
from pathlib import Path

import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification

def parse_args() -> argparse.Namespace:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Run inference with a saved custom classifier.")
    parser.add_argument("--model-dir", type=Path, required=True, help="Directory containing model, tokenizer and label_map.json")
    parser.add_argument("--text", type=str, required=True, help="Input text to classify")
    parser.add_argument("--max-length", type=int, default=128)
    return parser.parse_args()

def main() -> None:
    args = parse_args()
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(args.model_dir, use_fast=True)
    model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(args.model_dir)
    model.eval()
    with open(args.model_dir / "label_map.json", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        id2label = json.load(f)
    inputs = tokenizer(
        args.text,
        truncation=True,
        padding="max_length",
        max_length=args.max_length,
        return_tensors="pt",
    )
    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(**inputs)
        pred_id = torch.argmax(outputs.logits, dim=1).item()
    print(id2label[str(pred_id)])

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

# --- tests/test_dataset.py ---
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd
import pytest
from custom_text_classifier.dataset import TextClassificationDataset
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

@pytest.fixture
def tiny_csv(tmp_path: Path) -> Path:
    df = pd.DataFrame({"text": ["hello world", "goodbye"], "label": ["pos", "neg"]})
    csv_path = tmp_path / "data.csv"
    df.to_csv(csv_path, index=False)
    return csv_path

def test_dataset_loads(tiny_csv: Path) -> None:
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("distilbert-base-uncased", use_fast=True)
    ds = TextClassificationDataset(tiny_csv, tokenizer, max_length=16)
    assert len(ds) == 2
    assert ds.label2id == {"neg": 0, "pos": 1}
    sample = ds[0]
    assert "input_ids" in sample and sample["input_ids"].shape[0] == 16
    assert isinstance(sample["labels"].item(), int)

# --- tests/test_train.py ---
import shutil
from pathlib import Path
import pytest
from custom_text_classifier.config import TrainConfig

def test_training_runs(tiny_csv, tmp_path: Path) -> None:
    output_dir = tmp_path / "model"
    config = TrainConfig(
        train_path=tiny_csv,
        val_path=None,
        model_name="distilbert-base-uncased",
        num_epochs=1,
        batch_size=2,
        learning_rate=5e-5,
        max_length=32,
        output_dir=output_dir,
    )
    def fake_parse_args() -> TrainConfig:
        return config
    import custom_text_classifier.train as train_mod
    train_mod.parse_args = fake_parse_args
    train_mod.main()
    assert (output_dir / "pytorch_model.bin").exists()
    assert (output_dir / "label_map.json").exists()
    shutil.rmtree(output_dir)

# --- tests/test_infer.py ---
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path
import pytest

def test_inference_on_tiny_model(tmp_path: Path, tiny_csv):
    model_dir = tmp_path / "model"
    # Train tiny model first
    from custom_text_classifier.train import main as train_main
    from custom_text_classifier.config import TrainConfig
    config = TrainConfig(
        train_path=tiny_csv,
        val_path=None,
        model_name="distilbert-base-uncased",
        num_epochs=1,
        batch_size=2,
        learning_rate=5e-5,
        max_length=32,
        output_dir=model_dir,
    )
    def fake_parse():
        return config
    import custom_text_classifier.train as train_mod
    train_mod.parse_args = fake_parse
    train_mod.main()
    # Run inference CLI
    result = subprocess.run(
        [sys.executable, "-m", "custom_text_classifier.infer", "--model-dir", str(model_dir), "--text", "hello world"],
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    )
    assert result.returncode == 0
    predicted = result.stdout.strip()
    assert predicted in {"pos", "neg"}

exclude = tests

# --- scripts/type_check.sh ---

python -m mypy src

# --- scripts/build_wheel.sh ---
#!/usr/bin/env bash
python -m pip install --upgrade build
python -m build --sdist --wheel

# --- scripts/benchmark_inference.py ---
import time
from pathlib import Path
import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification

MODEL_DIR = Path("model")
TEXT = "This is a benchmark sentence."

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_DIR, use_fast=True)
model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(MODEL_DIR)
model.eval()
inputs = tokenizer(TEXT, return_tensors="pt", truncation=True, padding="max_length", max_length=128)
# Warm‑up
for _ in range(5):
    _ = model(**inputs)
start = time.time()
for _ in range(100):
    _ = model(**inputs)
elapsed = time.time() - start
print(f"Average inference time: {elapsed/100*1000:.2f} ms")

# --- .github/workflows/ci.yml ---
name: CI
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          python -m venv .venv
          source .venv/bin/activate
          pip install --upgrade pip
          pip install -e .[dev]
      - name: Run tests
        run: |
          source .venv/bin/activate
          pytest -q
      - name: Type check
        run: |
          source .venv/bin/activate
          python -m mypy src
how_to_run:
```bash
# 1️⃣ Clone the repo and create a virtual environment
git clone https://github.com/yourname/custom_text_classifier.git
cd custom_text_classifier
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate

# 2️⃣ Install the package with development extras
pip install -e .[dev]

# 3️⃣ Prepare a CSV `train.csv` with columns `text` and `label`
#    (optional `val.csv` for validation)

# 4️⃣ Train the model
train-model \
  --train-path train.csv \
  --val-path val.csv \
  --model-name distilbert-base-uncased \
  --epochs 3 \
  --batch-size 32 \
  --output-dir model

# 5️⃣ Run inference on a new sentence
infer-model --model-dir model --text "I love this product!"
```

The `model/` folder will contain `pytorch_model.bin`, `config.json`, the tokenizer files and `label_map.json`. Use the `scripts/benchmark_inference.py` to measure latency, and run `pytest -q` to execute the test suite.

challenges.title: Add Early Stopping
challenges.description: Modify the training loop to stop early if validation loss does not improve for a configurable number of epochs.
challenges.build_process:
1. Extend `TrainConfig` with `early_stopping_patience: int = 2`.
2. Track the best validation loss after each epoch.
3. If the loss hasn't improved for `patience` epochs, break the training loop.
4. Save the best model checkpoint separately (e.g., `best_model/`).
challenges.solution_code:
```python
# Add to TrainConfig in config.py
early_stopping_patience: int = 2

# Inside the training loop in train.py (replace the epoch loop)
best_val_loss = float('inf')
patience_counter = 0
for epoch in range(1, config.num_epochs + 1):
    # ... training part unchanged ...
    if val_loader:
        model.eval()
        val_losses = []
        with torch.no_grad():
            for batch in val_loader:
                batch = {k: v for k, v in batch.items()}
                outputs = model(**batch)
                val_losses.append(outputs.loss.item())
        avg_val_loss = sum(val_losses) / len(val_losses)
        logger.info(f"Validation loss: {avg_val_loss:.4f}")
        if avg_val_loss < best_val_loss:
            best_val_loss = avg_val_loss
            patience_counter = 0
            # Save best checkpoint
            best_dir = config.output_dir / "best_model"
            best_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
            model.save_pretrained(best_dir)
            tokenizer.save_pretrained(best_dir)
            with open(best_dir / "label_map.json", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
                json.dump(train_dataset.id2label, f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)
            logger.info("New best model saved.")
        else:
            patience_counter += 1
            logger.info(f"No improvement, patience {patience_counter}/{config.early_stopping_patience}")
            if patience_counter >= config.early_stopping_patience:
                logger.info("Early stopping triggered.")
                break
```
challenges.solution_walkthrough: We first extend the configuration with a `early_stopping_patience` field so the user can control how many epochs without improvement are tolerated. Inside the epoch loop we compute the average validation loss after each epoch. If the current loss is lower than the best seen so far, we reset the patience counter, update `best_val_loss`, and write a checkpoint to `best_model/`. If the loss does not improve, we increment the counter; once it reaches the patience limit we break out of the training loop, effectively stopping early. This logic keeps the training fast on noisy datasets while still preserving the best performing model.
